channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Dwarven Forge Review - TTRPG Terrain To Enhance, But Not To Fully Replace
item.content:encoded:
Since I began playing Dungeons & Dragons, I've always been looking for the best way to represent 3D spaces when I put out a battle map in front of my parties. Like everyone, I started with pen and paper, and erasable grid boards, but Dwarven Forge was always in my line of sight. Biting the bullet last year, I've now gotten plenty of experience crafting combat encounters and learning the pros and cons of this top-of-the-line TTRGP terrain.

Dwarven Forge is high-fidelity modular terrain for Dungeons & Dragons, and really any TTRPG. In its most basic form, Dwarven Forge is pieces of 2x2 terrain pieces that comprise floors, walls, doorways, and more. With magnets on their bases, you can attach them to a Terrain Tray to hold them in place as you build up a map like LEGOs.

Dwarven Forge Is The Perfect Level Of Specificity

To accommodate the different settings that TTRPG adventures might take place in, pieces are themed generically after dungeons, cities, caves, and even in sci-fi settings. The Dwarven Forge pieces do just enough to set the scene, without getting too specific that they lose their versatility.

Past the basics, the Dwarven Forge catalogue lets you fill your maps with traps, scenery, and even LED lights that glow ominously or flicker like fire. This can get really specific as you're browsing magnetic store signs, a tool cabinet, or a boarded-up window.

Starting with the basics, I obtained a starter set dungeon and cave system, but it was a very slippery slope as I saw more beautiful pieces to start filling up my world with to draw my players further in.

I have absolutely fallen in love with part of my weekly D&D prep being reviewing upcoming battle maps, figuring out how I can best translate the 2D map into Dwarven Forge, and even how I can enhance it by adding more verticality to a battlefield that can't as easily be conveyed if we were just playing on a dry-erase board.

Verticality is one of the largest benefits of Dwarven Forge. No longer being limited to 2D movement or imagining how different terrain adds verticality, Dwarven Forge lets you create unique maps with ease. Filling a map with verticality, towers, and props also lets your players be more creative about the presence of objects to hide behind or how they can use the terrain to their advantage. The days of 5 by 6 inch empty boxes is over.

Map Prep Becomes More Important

While there's a lot that Dwarven Forge has going for it, there are certainly also a number of downsides. When running a game from a book with a battle map in front of me, the prep time was definitely reduced. I could see the image and know that's what needed to get drawn out. Now, creating a battlemap takes preparation, while not a lot, it's still definitely time that's added to prep.

This crafting of a map also means you need to know when it's coming, and hope that your players don't craft a plan for combat to occur in a totally different setting. Shortly after obtaining my first shipment, the party was scheduled to have an encounter with two assassins in a cave while escaping a walled town. The cave was created, and the stage was set… so I was left scrambling when invisibility and tricky teleportation magic determined their approach to leaving the city was through the front gate.

Premium, But Still Downsides

The map sitting behind the scenes was left untouched as I quickly defaulted back to my battle map and markers to set a totally new scene.

Another aspect of Dwarven Forge that is hard to gloss over is the price point. This is certainly the premium in TTRPG terrain, and it brings with is a more expensive pricetag. Having always looked for the best battle maps to fit my table when I was only using a battle map, or Etsy-purchased linking walls, there was always a sense of "these will get upgraded in the future." With Dwarven Forge, however, it feels like there isn't really anywhere to upgrade to.

It softens the blow of the purchase, knowing that these are terrain pieces that I'll get to put in front of players time and time again, no matter what TTRPG I'm playing.

Dwarven Forge Review | Final Thoughts

While Dwarven Forge stands head and shoulders above other options, it's clear it's not a fix for every situation that your game might encounter (unless you plan to purchase enough to build Castle Ravenloft). In using Dwarven Forge for my multiple games over the past year, it's been best utilized in conjunction with some of the tried and true battle map tools that forever DMs are more than familiar with.

There had been initial worry that maps built with Dwarven Forge might restrict gameplay too much. In practice, I found it was the exact opposite. By having such a clearly defined setting for combat, my players now spend less time asking about logistics or ignoring terrain in a room, and are instead using it to enhance the types of strategy they use in combat.

Dwarven Forge might have a luxury price tag attached to it, but for TTRPG pieces that you'll need to purchase once and never have to worry about again, it makes sense why they're the best.

Dwarven Forge was reviewed with product purchased by the reviewer over a year of near-weekly D&D games - All photos were taken by the reviewer

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew
